Member-only story

Cobol Programming

Programming

Java

Fintech

Floating Points

Floating-point math is fine for almost everything. It is not fine for money.

4 min read
Just now

--

In 1982, the Vancouver Stock Exchange accidentally proved that a rounding error can out-crash a crash.

• A new stock index launches at a clean 1,000.000 points.

• One line of code truncates instead of rounds — dropping the third decimal place, every single time.

• Nothing crashes. No alert fires. No one notices.

• The index just quietly bleeds ~25 points a month, silently, for 22 months straight.

• By the time anyone catches it, the market has lost more than half its value — on paper.

• They fix the one-line bug and recalculate overnight.

• The index jumps from 524.811 to 1098.892 — in a single tick.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• Two years of “market decline” undone in one recalculation. It was never the market. It was a rounding function.
Then it happened to us, in a system I helped design.
The Confession Part
I was the one who pushed to rewrite our interest calculation service. I said things like “single source of truth for EMI logic” and “no…
